metadata.title: Quick-fire: Which circle gets the call
metadata.author: patterns-of-choice editorial draft
metadata.design_intent: Companion to qf-ingroup-001. Different surface variations: workplace in-group/out-group at organization scale, online-vs-IRL friend prioritization, new colleague from different professional background, tourist-pricing differential (low-stakes universalism question), cultural-community traditional membership, family-of-origin vs chosen-family time commitment. Probes whether the user's in-group/out-group preferences are stable across kinds of community (workplace / online / cultural / familial), which is the same psychological construct but operationalized very differently per context.
metadata.domain_anchor: Cikara/Bruneau parochial-empathy work; Haidt MFT loyalty foundation; Brewer optimal-distinctiveness theory for the online-vs-IRL framing; cultural-belonging literature (Cohen's culture-of-honor; Cross & Madson interdependent self-construal).

items.prompt: Your team and another team at your company are competing for the same internal project. A colleague on the other team — someone you know reasonably well — asks for your candid take on their proposal.

options.text: Give the candid take; it's the right thing for them and the project.
Give a polite, non-substantive answer; that's the line you owe your team.

items.prompt: Saturday: a close online friend (you've never met in person, talked for years) is moving and asks for two hours of voice-call help debugging a stressful logistics situation. Your IRL friend group is hosting a casual brunch the same morning.

options.text: Skip brunch; the online friend has more concrete need today.
Go to brunch; in-person relationships need the recurring time.

items.prompt: A new teammate joined recently from a substantially different professional background. The team is making good-natured but pointed jokes about how their previous industry approaches things. The teammate is in the room and smiling thinly.

options.text: Push back lightly: "OK, but their old industry actually had a point about X."
Don't push back; it's not malicious and they'll find their footing.

items.prompt: Traveling. You notice a market stall is charging you about 50% more than the price a local just paid right in front of you. The amount difference is small ($3).

options.text: Pay the higher price; it's the local norm and the difference is small.
Push back: ask for the price you just saw the local pay.

items.prompt: Your cultural/religious community has a long tradition about who counts as a full member. A close friend (in a serious relationship with someone from your community) is interested in joining but doesn't meet the traditional criteria.

options.text: Advocate for them and against the tradition, even with community elders.
Respect the tradition; help your friend find an adjacent path that fits.

items.prompt: Same weekend: your family-of-origin asks for a major time commitment (extended visit). Your chosen-family (long-term partner / close friends not related by blood) asks for the same time block.

options.text: Family-of-origin first; biological family has different weight.
Chosen-family this time; the relationships I built deserve at least equal weight.
